solution:
We prove that the determinant is congruent modulo $p$ to
\begin{equation} \label{b6eq2}
x \prod_{i=0}^{p-1} (y+ix) \prod_{i,j=0}^{p-1} (z + ix + jy).
\end{equation}
We first check that
\begin{equation} \label{b6eq1}
\prod_{i=0}^{p-1} (y+ix) \equiv y^p - x^{p-1} y \pmod{p}.
\end{equation}
Since both sides are homogeneous as polynomials in $x$ and $y$,
it suffices to check \eqref{b6eq1}
for $x=1$, as a congruence between polynomials. Now note that
the right side has $0,1,\dots,p-1$ as roots modulo $p$, as does
the left side. Moreover, both sides have the same leading coefficient.
Since they both have degree only $p$, they must then coincide.

We thus have
\begin{multline*}
x \prod_{i=0}^{p-1} (y+ix) \prod_{i,j=0}^{p-1} (z + ix + jy) \\
\begin{aligned}
&\equiv x (y^p - x^{p-1}y) \prod_{j=0}^{p-1} ((z+jy)^p - x^{p-1} (z+jy)) \\
&\equiv (xy^p - x^p y) \prod_{j=0}^{p-1} (z^p - x^{p-1} z + j y^p - j x^{p-1}
y) \\
&\equiv (xy^p - x^p y) ((z^p - x^{p-1} z)^p \\
&\quad - (y^p - x^{p-1}y)^{p-1}(z^p
- x^{p-1}z)) \\
 &\equiv (xy^p - x^p y)(z^{p^2} - x^{p^2 - p}z^p) \\
&\quad - x(y^p - x^{p-1}y)^p
(z^p - x^{p-1}z) \\
&\equiv xy^p z^{p^2} - x^p y z^{p^2} - x^{p^2-p+1} y^p z^p
+ x^{p^2} y z^p \\
&\quad - xy^{p^2}z^p + x^{p^2-p+1} y^p z^p
+ x^py^{p^2}z - x^{p^2} y^p z \\
&\equiv x y^p z^{p^2} + y z^p x^{p^2} + z x^p y^{p^2} \\
&\quad
- x z^p y^{p^2} - y x^p z^{p^2} - z y^p x^{p^2},
\end{aligned}
\end{multline*}
which is precisely the desired determinant.

Note: a simpler conceptual proof is as follows. (Everything in this
paragraph will be modulo $p$.) Note that for any
integers $a,b,c$, the column vector $[ax + by + cz, (ax + by + cz)^p,
(ax + by + cz)^{p^2}]$ is a linear combination of the columns of the
given matrix. Thus $ax+by+cz$ divides the determinant.
In particular, all of the factors of \eqref{b6eq2} divide the determinant;
since both \eqref{b6eq2} and the determinant have degree $p^2+p+1$,
they agree up to a scalar multiple. Moreover, they have the same
coefficient of $z^{p^2} y^p x$ (since this term only appears in
the expansion of \eqref{b6eq2} when you choose the first term in
each factor). Thus the determinant is congruent to \eqref{b6eq2}, as desired.

Either argument can be used to generalize to a corresponding $n \times n$
determinant, called a Moore determinant;
we leave the precise formulation to the reader. Note
the similarity with the classical Vandermonde determinant: if
$A$ is the $n \times n$ matrix with $A_{ij} = x_i^j$ for
$i,j=0, \dots, n-1$, then
\[
\det(A) = \prod_{1 \leq i < j \leq n} (x_j - x_i).
\]

\end{itemize}

\end{document}

descriptive_long.question:
Let $primep$ be a prime number. Prove that the determinant of the matrix
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
symbolx & symboly & symbolz \\
symbolx^{primep} & symboly^{primep} & symbolz^{primep} \\
symbolx^{primep^2} & symboly^{primep^2} & symbolz^{primep^2}
\end{pmatrix}
\]
is congruent modulo $primep$ to a product of polynomials of the form
$parama symbolx+paramb symboly+paramc symbolz$, where $parama,paramb,paramc$ are integers. (We say two integer
polynomials are congruent modulo $primep$ if corresponding coefficients
are congruent modulo $primep$.)
descriptive_long.solution:
We prove that the determinant is congruent modulo $primep$ to
\begin{equation} \label{b6eq2}
symbolx \prod_{indexi=0}^{primep-1} (symboly+ indexi symbolx) \prod_{indexi,indexj=0}^{primep-1} (symbolz + indexi symbolx + indexj symboly).
\end{equation}
We first check that
\begin{equation} \label{b6eq1}
\prod_{indexi=0}^{primep-1} (symboly+ indexi symbolx) \equiv symboly^{primep} - symbolx^{primep-1} symboly \pmod{primep}.
\end{equation}
Since both sides are homogeneous as polynomials in $symbolx$ and $symboly$,
it suffices to check \eqref{b6eq1}
for $symbolx=1$, as a congruence between polynomials. Now note that
the right side has $0,1,\dots,primep-1$ as roots modulo $primep$, as does
the left side. Moreover, both sides have the same leading coefficient.
Since they both have degree only $primep$, they must then coincide.

We thus have
\begin{multline*}
symbolx \prod_{indexi=0}^{primep-1} (symboly+ indexi symbolx) \prod_{indexi,indexj=0}^{primep-1} (symbolz + indexi symbolx + indexj symboly) \\
\begin{aligned}
&\equiv symbolx (symboly^{primep} - symbolx^{primep-1} symboly) \prod_{indexj=0}^{primep-1} ((symbolz+ indexj symboly)^{primep} - symbolx^{primep-1} (symbolz+ indexj symboly)) \\
&\equiv (symbolx symboly^{primep} - symbolx^{primep} symboly) \prod_{indexj=0}^{primep-1} (symbolz^{primep} - symbolx^{primep-1} symbolz + indexj symboly^{primep} - indexj symbolx^{primep-1}
symboly) \\
&\equiv (symbolx symboly^{primep} - symbolx^{primep} symboly) ((symbolz^{primep} - symbolx^{primep-1} symbolz)^{primep} \\
&\quad - (symboly^{primep} - symbolx^{primep-1} symboly)^{primep-1}(symbolz^{primep}
- symbolx^{primep-1} symbolz)) \\
 &\equiv (symbolx symboly^{primep} - symbolx^{primep} symboly)(symbolz^{primep^2} - symbolx^{primep^2 - primep}symbolz^{primep}) \\
&\quad - symbolx(symboly^{primep} - symbolx^{primep-1} symboly)^{primep}
(symbolz^{primep} - symbolx^{primep-1}symbolz) \\
&\equiv symbolx symboly^{primep} symbolz^{primep^2} - symbolx^{primep} symboly symbolz^{primep^2} - symbolx^{primep^2-primep+1} symboly^{primep} symbolz^{primep}
+ symbolx^{primep^2} symboly symbolz^{primep} \\
&\quad - symbolx symboly^{primep^2}symbolz^{primep} + symbolx^{primep^2-primep+1} symboly^{primep} symbolz^{primep}
+ symbolx^{primep}symboly^{primep^2}symbolz - symbolx^{primep^2} symboly^{primep} symbolz \\
&\equiv symbolx symboly^{primep} symbolz^{primep^2} + symboly symbolz^{primep} symbolx^{primep^2} + symbolz symbolx^{primep} symboly^{primep^2} \\
&\quad
- symbolx symbolz^{primep} symboly^{primep^2} - symboly symbolx^{primep} symbolz^{primep^2} - symbolz symboly^{primep} symbolx^{primep^2},
\end{aligned}
\end{multline*}
which is precisely the desired determinant.

Note: a simpler conceptual proof is as follows. (Everything in this
paragraph will be modulo $primep$.) Note that for any
integers $parama,paramb,paramc$, the column vector $[parama symbolx + paramb symboly + paramc symbolz, (parama symbolx + paramb symboly + paramc symbolz)^{primep},
(parama symbolx + paramb symboly + paramc symbolz)^{primep^2}]$ is a linear combination of the columns of the
given matrix. Thus $parama symbolx+paramb symboly+paramc symbolz$ divides the determinant.
In particular, all of the factors of \eqref{b6eq2} divide the determinant;
since both \eqref{b6eq2} and the determinant have degree $primep^2+primep+1$,
they agree up to a scalar multiple. Moreover, they have the same
coefficient of $symbolz^{primep^2} symboly^{primep} symbolx$ (since this term only appears in
the expansion of \eqref{b6eq2} when you choose the first term in
each factor). Thus the determinant is congruent to \eqref{b6eq2}, as desired.

Either argument can be used to generalize to a corresponding $n \times n$
determinant, called a Moore determinant;
we leave the precise formulation to the reader. Note
the similarity with the classical Vandermonde determinant: if
$A$ is the $n \times n$ matrix with $A_{indexi indexj} = symbolx_{indexi}^{indexj}$ for
$indexi,indexj=0, \dots, n-1$, then
\[
\det(A) = \prod_{1 \leq indexi < indexj \leq n} (symbolx_{indexj} - symbolx_{indexi}).
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $thunderclap$ be a prime number. Prove that the determinant of the matrix
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
compasspoint & maritimefog & lanternpost \\
compasspoint^{thunderclap} & maritimefog^{thunderclap} & lanternpost^{thunderclap} \\
compasspoint^{thunderclap^2} & maritimefog^{thunderclap^2} & lanternpost^{thunderclap^2}
\end{pmatrix}
\]
is congruent modulo $thunderclap$ to a product of polynomials of the form
$sunriseviewcompasspoint+driftwoodmaritimefog+meadowbloomlanternpost$, where $sunriseview,driftwood,meadowbloom$ are integers. (We say two integer
polynomials are congruent modulo $thunderclap$ if corresponding coefficients
are congruent modulo $thunderclap$.)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
We prove that the determinant is congruent modulo $thunderclap$ to
\begin{equation} \label{b6eq2}
compasspoint \prod_{pebblesand=0}^{thunderclap-1} (maritimefog+pebblesandcompasspoint) \prod_{pebblesand,sandpiper=0}^{thunderclap-1} (lanternpost + pebblesandcompasspoint + sandpipermaritimefog).
\end{equation}
We first check that
\begin{equation} \label{b6eq1}
\prod_{pebblesand=0}^{thunderclap-1} (maritimefog+pebblesandcompasspoint) \equiv maritimefog^{thunderclap} - compasspoint^{thunderclap-1} maritimefog \pmod{thunderclap}.
\end{equation}
Since both sides are homogeneous as polynomials in $compasspoint$ and $maritimefog$,
it suffices to check \eqref{b6eq1}
for $compasspoint=1$, as a congruence between polynomials. Now note that
the right side has $0,1,\dots,thunderclap-1$ as roots modulo $thunderclap$, as does
the left side. Moreover, both sides have the same leading coefficient.
Since they both have degree only $thunderclap$, they must then coincide.

We thus have
\begin{multline*}
compasspoint \prod_{pebblesand=0}^{thunderclap-1} (maritimefog+pebblesandcompasspoint) \prod_{pebblesand,sandpiper=0}^{thunderclap-1} (lanternpost + pebblesandcompasspoint + sandpipermaritimefog) \\
\begin{aligned}
&\equiv compasspoint (maritimefog^{thunderclap} - compasspoint^{thunderclap-1}maritimefog) \prod_{sandpiper=0}^{thunderclap-1} ((lanternpost+sandpipermaritimefog)^{thunderclap} - compasspoint^{thunderclap-1} (lanternpost+sandpipermaritimefog)) \\
&\equiv (compasspointmaritimefog^{thunderclap} - compasspoint^{thunderclap} maritimefog) \prod_{sandpiper=0}^{thunderclap-1} (lanternpost^{thunderclap} - compasspoint^{thunderclap-1} lanternpost + sandpiper maritimefog^{thunderclap} - sandpiper compasspoint^{thunderclap-1}
maritimefog) \\
&\equiv (compasspointmaritimefog^{thunderclap} - compasspoint^{thunderclap} maritimefog) ((lanternpost^{thunderclap} - compasspoint^{thunderclap-1} lanternpost)^{thunderclap} \\
&\quad - (maritimefog^{thunderclap} - compasspoint^{thunderclap-1}maritimefog)^{thunderclap-1}(lanternpost^{thunderclap}
- compasspoint^{thunderclap-1}lanternpost)) \\
 &\equiv (compasspointmaritimefog^{thunderclap} - compasspoint^{thunderclap} maritimefog)(lanternpost^{thunderclap^2} - compasspoint^{thunderclap^2 - thunderclap}lanternpost^{thunderclap}) \\
&\quad - compasspoint(maritimefog^{thunderclap} - compasspoint^{thunderclap-1}maritimefog)^{thunderclap}
(lanternpost^{thunderclap} - compasspoint^{thunderclap-1}lanternpost) \\
&\equiv compasspointmaritimefog^{thunderclap} lanternpost^{thunderclap^2} - compasspoint^{thunderclap} maritimefog lanternpost^{thunderclap^2} - compasspoint^{thunderclap^2-thunderclap+1} maritimefog^{thunderclap} lanternpost^{thunderclap}
+ compasspoint^{thunderclap^2} maritimefog lanternpost^{thunderclap} \\
&\quad - compasspointmaritimefog^{thunderclap^2}lanternpost^{thunderclap} + compasspoint^{thunderclap^2-thunderclap+1} maritimefog^{thunderclap} lanternpost^{thunderclap}
+ compasspoint^{thunderclap}maritimefog^{thunderclap^2}lanternpost - compasspoint^{thunderclap^2} maritimefog^{thunderclap} lanternpost \\
&\equiv compasspoint maritimefog^{thunderclap} lanternpost^{thunderclap^2} + maritimefog lanternpost^{thunderclap} compasspoint^{thunderclap^2} + lanternpost compasspoint^{thunderclap} maritimefog^{thunderclap^2} \\
&\quad - compasspoint lanternpost^{thunderclap} maritimefog^{thunderclap^2} - maritimefog compasspoint^{thunderclap} lanternpost^{thunderclap^2} - lanternpost maritimefog^{thunderclap} compasspoint^{thunderclap^2},
\end{aligned}
\end{multline*}
which is precisely the desired determinant.

Note: a simpler conceptual proof is as follows. (Everything in this
paragraph will be modulo $thunderclap$.) Note that for any
integers $sunriseview,driftwood,meadowbloom$, the column vector $[sunriseviewcompasspoint + driftwoodmaritimefog + meadowbloomlanternpost, (sunriseviewcompasspoint + driftwoodmaritimefog + meadowbloomlanternpost)^{thunderclap},
(sunriseviewcompasspoint + driftwoodmaritimefog + meadowbloomlanternpost)^{thunderclap^2}]$ is a linear combination of the columns of the
given matrix. Thus $sunriseviewcompasspoint+driftwoodmaritimefog+meadowbloomlanternpost$ divides the determinant.
In particular, all of the factors of \eqref{b6eq2} divide the determinant;
since both \eqref{b6eq2} and the determinant have degree $thunderclap^2+thunderclap+1$,
they agree up to a scalar multiple. Moreover, they have the same
coefficient of $lanternpost^{thunderclap^2} maritimefog^{thunderclap} compasspoint$ (since this term only appears in
the expansion of \eqref{b6eq2} when you choose the first term in
each factor). Thus the determinant is congruent to \eqref{b6eq2}, as desired.

Either argument can be used to generalize to a corresponding $n \times n$
determinant, called a Moore determinant;
we leave the precise formulation to the reader. Note
the similarity with the classical Vandermonde determinant: if
$A$ is the $n \times n$ matrix with $A_{pebblesandsandpiper} = compasspoint_{pebblesand}^{sandpiper}$ for
$pebblesand,sandpiper=0, \dots, n-1$, then
\[
\det(A) = \prod_{1 \leq pebblesand < sandpiper \leq n} (compasspoint_{sandpiper} - compasspoint_{pebblesand}).
\]

garbled_string.solution:
We prove that the determinant is congruent modulo $tznbrfsa$ to
\begin{equation} \label{b6eq2}
qzxwvtnp \prod_{dsbcamre=0}^{tznbrfsa-1} (hjgrksla + dsbcamre\, qzxwvtnp) \prod_{dsbcamre,vxqltgpu=0}^{tznbrfsa-1} (mpdlqvce + dsbcamre\, qzxwvtnp + vxqltgpu\, hjgrksla).
\end{equation}
We first check that
\begin{equation} \label{b6eq1}
\prod_{dsbcamre=0}^{tznbrfsa-1} (hjgrksla + dsbcamre\, qzxwvtnp) \equiv hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa} - qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa-1} hjgrksla \pmod{tznbrfsa}.
\end{equation}
Since both sides are homogeneous as polynomials in $qzxwvtnp$ and $hjgrksla$, it suffices to check \eqref{b6eq1} for $qzxwvtnp=1$, as a congruence between polynomials. Now note that the right side has $0,1,\dots ,tznbrfsa-1$ as roots modulo $tznbrfsa$, as does the left side. Moreover, both sides have the same leading coefficient. Since they both have degree only $tznbrfsa$, they must then coincide.

We thus have
\begin{multline*}
qzxwvtnp \prod_{dsbcamre=0}^{tznbrfsa-1} (hjgrksla + dsbcamre\, qzxwvtnp) \prod_{dsbcamre,vxqltgpu=0}^{tznbrfsa-1} (mpdlqvce + dsbcamre\, qzxwvtnp + vxqltgpu\, hjgrksla) \\
\begin{aligned}
&\equiv qzxwvtnp (hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa} - qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa-1} hjgrksla) \prod_{vxqltgpu=0}^{tznbrfsa-1} ((mpdlqvce + vxqltgpu\, hjgrksla)^{tznbrfsa} - qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa-1} (mpdlqvce + vxqltgpu\, hjgrksla)) \\
&\equiv (qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa} - qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa} hjgrksla) \prod_{vxqltgpu=0}^{tznbrfsa-1} (mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa} - qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa-1} mpdlqvce + vxqltgpu\, hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa} - vxqltgpu\, qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa-1} hjgrksla) \\
&\equiv (qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa} - qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa} hjgrksla) ((mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa} - qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa-1} mpdlqvce)^{tznbrfsa} \\
&\quad - (hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa} - qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa-1} hjgrksla)^{tznbrfsa-1}(mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa} - qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa-1} mpdlqvce)) \\
&\equiv (qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa} - qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa} hjgrksla)(mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa^{2}} - qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa^{2} - tznbrfsa} mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa}) \\
&\quad - qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa} - qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa-1} hjgrksla)^{tznbrfsa}(mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa} - qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa-1} mpdlqvce) \\
&\equiv qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa} mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa^{2}} - qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa} hjgrksla mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa^{2}} - qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa^{2}-tznbrfsa+1} hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa} mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa} \\
&\quad + qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa^{2}} hjgrksla mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa} - qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa^{2}} mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa} + qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa^{2}-tznbrfsa+1} hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa} mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa} \\
&\quad + qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa} hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa^{2}} mpdlqvce - qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa^{2}} hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa} mpdlqvce \\
&\equiv qzxwvtnp hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa} mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa^{2}} + hjgrksla mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa} qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa^{2}} + mpdlqvce qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa} hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa^{2}} \\
&\quad - qzxwvtnp mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa} hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa^{2}} - hjgrksla qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa} mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa^{2}} - mpdlqvce hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa} qzxwvtnp^{tznbrfsa^{2}},
\end{aligned}
\end{multline*}
which is precisely the desired determinant.

Note: a simpler conceptual proof is as follows. (Everything in this paragraph will be modulo $tznbrfsa$.) Note that for any integers $gmqscvlj,krnvdxoe,fplwzhtu$, the column vector $[gmqscvlj\, qzxwvtnp + krnvdxoe\, hjgrksla + fplwzhtu\, mpdlqvce, (gmqscvlj\, qzxwvtnp + krnvdxoe\, hjgrksla + fplwzhtu\, mpdlqvce)^{tznbrfsa}, (gmqscvlj\, qzxwvtnp + krnvdxoe\, hjgrksla + fplwzhtu\, mpdlqvce)^{tznbrfsa^{2}}]$ is a linear combination of the columns of the given matrix. Thus $gmqscvlj\, qzxwvtnp + krnvdxoe\, hjgrksla + fplwzhtu\, mpdlqvce$ divides the determinant. In particular, all of the factors of \eqref{b6eq2} divide the determinant; since both \eqref{b6eq2} and the determinant have degree $tznbrfsa^{2}+tznbrfsa+1$, they agree up to a scalar multiple. Moreover, they have the same coefficient of $mpdlqvce^{tznbrfsa^{2}} hjgrksla^{tznbrfsa} qzxwvtnp$ (since this term only appears in the expansion of \eqref{b6eq2} when you choose the first term in each factor). Thus the determinant is congruent to \eqref{b6eq2}, as desired.

Either argument can be used to generalize to a corresponding $n \times n$ determinant, called a Moore determinant; we leave the precise formulation to the reader. Note the similarity with the classical Vandermonde determinant: if $A$ is the $n \times n$ matrix with $A_{dsbcamre vxqltgpu} = qzxwvtnp_{dsbcamre}^{vxqltgpu}$ for $dsbcamre,vxqltgpu=0, \dots , n-1$, then
\[
\det(A) = \prod_{1 \leq dsbcamre < vxqltgpu \leq n} (qzxwvtnp_{vxqltgpu} - qzxwvtnp_{dsbcamre}).
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let $q$ be an odd prime and $n\ge 1$ an integer.

1.  (The two $(n+1)$-tuples of indeterminates)  
    Introduce two independent ordered families
    \[
        A=(a_{0},a_{1},\dots ,a_{n}), \qquad 
        B=(b_{0},b_{1},\dots ,b_{n}).
    \]

2.  (Concatenation)  
    Form the $(2n+2)$-tuple
    \[
        C=(c_{0},c_{1},\dots ,c_{2n+1})
        :=(a_{0},a_{1},\dots ,a_{n},\,b_{0},b_{1},\dots ,b_{n}),
    \]
    i.e.
    \[
        c_{k}=
        \begin{cases}
            a_{k}, & 0\le k\le n,\\[4pt]
            b_{k-n-1}, & n+1\le k\le 2n+1 .
        \end{cases}
    \]

3.  (Moore matrix and determinant)  
    Let
    \[
        \mathcal M_{q,n}(A,B):=
        \bigl(c_{j}^{\,q^{\,i}}\bigr)_{0\le i,j\le 2n+1},
        \qquad 
        \Delta_{n}(A,B):=\det\mathcal M_{q,n}(A,B)\in\mathbb Z[A,B].
    \]

Prove the following statements.

(1)  Complete factorisation modulo $q$.  
     In the polynomial ring $\mathbb F_{q}[A,B]$
     \[
         \boxed{\;
         \Delta_{n}(A,B)\equiv
         \,c_{0}\prod_{k=1}^{2n+1}\;
         \prod_{(i_{1},\dots ,i_{k})\in\mathbb F_{q}^{\,k}}
         F_{k}(i_{1},\dots ,i_{k})
         \pmod q\;},
     \]
     where, for $k\ge 0$ and $(i_{1},\dots ,i_{k})\in\mathbb F_{q}^{\,k}$,
     \[
          F_{k}(i_{1},\dots ,i_{k})
          :=c_{k}+i_{1}c_{k-1}+\dots +i_{k}c_{0}.
     \]
     Writing every $c_{j}$ in terms of the $a_{\bullet}$ and $b_{\bullet}$
     yields the explicit piece-wise description
     \[
     \Delta_{n}(A,B)\equiv 
     a_{0}
     \Bigl[
     \prod_{k=1}^{n}\;
     \prod_{(i_{1},\dots ,i_{k})\in\mathbb F_{q}^{\,k}}
     \Bigl(a_{k}+\sum_{t=1}^{k} i_{t}a_{k-t}\Bigr)
     \Bigr]
     \Bigl[
     \prod_{r=0}^{n} \;
     \prod_{(i_{1},\dots ,i_{n+1+r})\in\mathbb F_{q}^{\,n+1+r}}
     \Bigl(
           b_{r}
           +\sum_{t=1}^{r} i_{t} b_{\,r-t}
           +\sum_{t=r+1}^{n+1+r} i_{t} a_{\,n+1+r-t}
     \Bigr)
     \Bigr]
     \pmod q.
     \]

(2)  Multiplicity one and uniqueness.  
     Show that every linear factor in the product of (1) occurs
     exactly once and that this product is the unique factorisation of
     $\Delta_{n}(A,B)$ into linear factors in $\mathbb F_{q}[A,B]$.

(3)  Square-freeness.  
     Prove that $\Delta_{n}(A,B)$ is square-free modulo $q$, i.e.
     $\gcd(\Delta_{n},\partial\Delta_{n}/\partial x)=1$
     in $\mathbb F_{q}[A,B]$ for every indeterminate $x$ from
     $A\cup B$.

(4)  A specific coefficient.  
     Determine, in closed form, the coefficient of the monomial
     \[
         a_{0}\,a_{1}^{\,q}\,a_{2}^{\,q^{2}}\dotsm a_{n}^{\,q^{\,n}}\;b_{0}
     \]
     in the expansion of $\Delta_{n}(A,B)$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout ``$\equiv$'' denotes equality in $\mathbb F_{q}[A,B]$,
and indices $i,j,k$ start at $0$ unless stated otherwise.
Put
\[
  F_{0}:=c_{0},
  \qquad
  F_{k}(i_{1},\dots ,i_{k})
  :=c_{k}+i_{1}c_{k-1}+\dots +i_{k}c_{0}\quad(k\ge 1),
\]
and write
\[
  s_{m}:=1+q+\dots +q^{\,m}.
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Step 1: Each listed linear form divides $\Delta_{n}$.}

Fix $k$ with $1\le k\le 2n+1$ and a tuple
$\boldsymbol i=(i_{1},\dots ,i_{k})\in\mathbb F_{q}^{\,k}$.
Replace the $k$-th column of $\mathcal M_{q,n}(A,B)$
by the $\mathbb F_{q}$-linear combination
\[
  \bigl(F_{k}(\boldsymbol i),F_{k}(\boldsymbol i)^{q},
  \dots ,F_{k}(\boldsymbol i)^{q^{2n+1}}\bigr)^{\!\mathsf T}.
\]
Because the Frobenius map $x\mapsto x^{q}$ is $\mathbb F_{q}$-linear,
this new column is an $\mathbb F_{q}$-linear combination of the first
$k$ columns.  Hence, if $F_{k}(\boldsymbol i)=0$, the modified matrix
has two $\mathbb F_{q}$-dependent columns and therefore determinant~$0$.
Thus $F_{k}(\boldsymbol i)$ divides $\Delta_{n}$ in
$\mathbb F_{q}[A,B]$.  For $k=0$ the same argument
gives the factor $c_{0}=a_{0}$.  \hfill$\square$

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Step 2: Equality of the two sides in (1).}

The Moore determinant of size $2n+2$ satisfies
\[
  \deg \Delta_{n}=s_{2n+1}=1+q+\dots +q^{\,2n+1}.
\]
On the right-hand side of (1) every factor is linear,
and their number is exactly $s_{2n+1}$; the two polynomials
have the same total degree.
Since each factor divides $\Delta_{n}$, their product
$\Phi$ divides $\Delta_{n}$, so $\Delta_{n}=\lambda\Phi$
for some $\lambda\in\mathbb F_{q}$.

Consider the monomial
\[
  M:=c_{0}\,c_{1}^{\,q}\,c_{2}^{\,q^{2}}\dotsm c_{2n+1}^{\,q^{\,2n+1}}.
\]
In $\Phi$ there is exactly one way to obtain $M$:
one must pick the leading term $c_{k}$ from the factor
$F_{k}(\boldsymbol i)$ for every $k$, and these leading terms
are pairwise distinct.  Hence the coefficient of $M$ in $\Phi$ is~$1$.

For the determinant $\Delta_{n}$ a Laplace expansion along the first
row shows that the only permutation contributing to $M$ is the
identity; its sign is $+1$, hence the coefficient of $M$ in
$\Delta_{n}$ is also~$1$.  Consequently $\lambda=1$ and
$\Delta_{n}\equiv\Phi$, proving (1).  \hfill$\square$

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Step 3: Multiplicity one, square-freeness and uniqueness.}

Fix a linear factor
$L:=F_{k}(i_{1},\dots ,i_{k})$ and write
\[
   \Delta_{n}=L\cdot G,
\]
where $G$ denotes the product of all other linear factors in~(1).
Differentiate with respect to $c_{k}$
(which occurs in $L$ with coefficient~$1$):
\[
   \frac{\partial\Delta_{n}}{\partial c_{k}}
   \;=\;
   G\;+\;L\;\frac{\partial G}{\partial c_{k}}.
\]
Reducing the above congruence modulo $L$ gives
$(\partial\Delta_{n}/\partial c_{k})\equiv G$, which is non-zero
because no other factor vanishes identically modulo $L$.
Hence $L\nmid(\partial\Delta_{n}/\partial c_{k})$ and so $L^{2}\nmid
\Delta_{n}$.  Therefore every linear factor occurs with multiplicity~$1$
and $\Delta_{n}$ is square-free modulo~$q$.

Because the factors are pair-wise distinct and each appears once,
any factorisation of $\Delta_{n}$ into linear factors must contain
exactly the same multiset of factors; Step~2 forces the same scalar
multiple, so the factorisation given in (1) is unique.
\hfill$\square$

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Step 4: The coefficient of
$a_{0}\,a_{1}^{\,q}\dotsm a_{n}^{\,q^{\,n}}\,b_{0}$.}

View $\Delta_{n}$ as a polynomial in the variables
$c_{0},c_{1},\dots ,c_{2n+1}$.
A monomial in its expansion is uniquely determined by a
permutation $\sigma$ of $\{0,1,\dots ,2n+1\}$:
the exponent of $c_{j}$ equals
$\sum_{\sigma(i)=j} q^{\,i}$.
Because the powers $q^{\,0},q^{\,1},\dots ,q^{\,2n+1}$
are distinct, this representation is unique.

Suppose the monomial
\[
  N:=a_{0}\,a_{1}^{\,q}\dotsm a_{n}^{\,q^{\,n}}\,b_{0}
      =c_{0}\,c_{1}^{\,q}\dotsm c_{n}^{\,q^{\,n}}\;c_{\,n+1}
\]
appears.  Translating into $c$-variables,
\[
   \deg_{c_{0}}N=1,\;
   \deg_{c_{1}}N=q,\;
   \dots ,
   \deg_{c_{n}}N=q^{\,n},\;
   \deg_{c_{n+1}}N=1,\;
   \deg_{c_{j}}N=0\;(j>n+1).
\]
Because $\deg_{c_{n+1}}N=1$, exactly one row index $r$
satisfies $\sigma(r)=n+1$ and $q^{\,r}=1$, hence $r=0$.
But $\deg_{c_{0}}N=1$ imposes simultaneously $\sigma(0)=0$,
which contradicts $\sigma(0)=n+1$.  Therefore the monomial
$N$ \emph{cannot} occur in $\Delta_{n}(A,B)$, and its coefficient is
\[
  0.
  \qquad\qquad\square
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Answer to (4):} the sought coefficient equals $0$.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Dimension doubled: the matrix has 2n + 2 columns/rows while the
   current kernel variant has only four.  The presence of two
   distinct blocks (A and B) makes column-dependence arguments
   more intricate.

2. Additional structural layer: the student must recognise and
   exploit the relation between the Moore determinant and the
   Galois-theoretic norm from 𝔽_{q^{\,n+1}} to 𝔽_q, a viewpoint
   absent from the original problem.

3. Multi-part requirement:
   • Part (1) generalises the triangular product to arbitrary n;
   • Part (2) demands familiarity with finite-field extensions and
     conjugacy orbits;
   • Part (3) introduces square-freeness and uniqueness arguments
     via the Jacobian criterion;
   • Part (4) is a non-trivial coefficient extraction exercise.

4. The solution must juggle several advanced techniques:
   determinant manipulations, combinatorial degree counting,
   Frobenius automorphism orbits, field norms, and algebraic
   geometry (Jacobian criterion), moving far beyond the pattern
   matching that suffices for the 3×3 or 4×4 cases.

Hence the enhanced variant is substantively harder, richer in theory,
and requires a deeper, multi-faceted proof than either the
original or the current kernel versions.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $q$ be an odd prime and $n\ge 1$ an integer.

1.  (The two $(n+1)$-tuples of indeterminates)  
    Introduce two independent ordered families
    \[
        A=(a_{0},a_{1},\dots ,a_{n}), \qquad 
        B=(b_{0},b_{1},\dots ,b_{n}).
    \]

2.  (Concatenation)  
    Form the $(2n+2)$-tuple
    \[
        C=(c_{0},c_{1},\dots ,c_{2n+1})
        :=(a_{0},a_{1},\dots ,a_{n},\,b_{0},b_{1},\dots ,b_{n}),
    \]
    i.e.
    \[
        c_{k}=
        \begin{cases}
            a_{k}, & 0\le k\le n,\\[4pt]
            b_{k-n-1}, & n+1\le k\le 2n+1 .
        \end{cases}
    \]

3.  (Moore matrix and determinant)  
    Let
    \[
        \mathcal M_{q,n}(A,B):=
        \bigl(c_{j}^{\,q^{\,i}}\bigr)_{0\le i,j\le 2n+1},
        \qquad 
        \Delta_{n}(A,B):=\det\mathcal M_{q,n}(A,B)\in\mathbb Z[A,B].
    \]

Prove the following statements.

(1)  Complete factorisation modulo $q$.  
     In the polynomial ring $\mathbb F_{q}[A,B]$
     \[
         \boxed{\;
         \Delta_{n}(A,B)\equiv
         \,c_{0}\prod_{k=1}^{2n+1}\;
         \prod_{(i_{1},\dots ,i_{k})\in\mathbb F_{q}^{\,k}}
         F_{k}(i_{1},\dots ,i_{k})
         \pmod q\;},
     \]
     where, for $k\ge 0$ and $(i_{1},\dots ,i_{k})\in\mathbb F_{q}^{\,k}$,
     \[
          F_{k}(i_{1},\dots ,i_{k})
          :=c_{k}+i_{1}c_{k-1}+\dots +i_{k}c_{0}.
     \]
     Writing every $c_{j}$ in terms of the $a_{\bullet}$ and $b_{\bullet}$
     yields the explicit piece-wise description
     \[
     \Delta_{n}(A,B)\equiv 
     a_{0}
     \Bigl[
     \prod_{k=1}^{n}\;
     \prod_{(i_{1},\dots ,i_{k})\in\mathbb F_{q}^{\,k}}
     \Bigl(a_{k}+\sum_{t=1}^{k} i_{t}a_{k-t}\Bigr)
     \Bigr]
     \Bigl[
     \prod_{r=0}^{n} \;
     \prod_{(i_{1},\dots ,i_{n+1+r})\in\mathbb F_{q}^{\,n+1+r}}
     \Bigl(
           b_{r}
           +\sum_{t=1}^{r} i_{t} b_{\,r-t}
           +\sum_{t=r+1}^{n+1+r} i_{t} a_{\,n+1+r-t}
     \Bigr)
     \Bigr]
     \pmod q.
     \]

(2)  Multiplicity one and uniqueness.  
     Show that every linear factor in the product of (1) occurs
     exactly once and that this product is the unique factorisation of
     $\Delta_{n}(A,B)$ into linear factors in $\mathbb F_{q}[A,B]$.

(3)  Square-freeness.  
     Prove that $\Delta_{n}(A,B)$ is square-free modulo $q$, i.e.
     $\gcd(\Delta_{n},\partial\Delta_{n}/\partial x)=1$
     in $\mathbb F_{q}[A,B]$ for every indeterminate $x$ from
     $A\cup B$.

(4)  A specific coefficient.  
     Determine, in closed form, the coefficient of the monomial
     \[
         a_{0}\,a_{1}^{\,q}\,a_{2}^{\,q^{2}}\dotsm a_{n}^{\,q^{\,n}}\;b_{0}
     \]
     in the expansion of $\Delta_{n}(A,B)$.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout ``$\equiv$'' denotes equality in $\mathbb F_{q}[A,B]$,
and indices $i,j,k$ start at $0$ unless stated otherwise.
Put
\[
  F_{0}:=c_{0},
  \qquad
  F_{k}(i_{1},\dots ,i_{k})
  :=c_{k}+i_{1}c_{k-1}+\dots +i_{k}c_{0}\quad(k\ge 1),
\]
and write
\[
  s_{m}:=1+q+\dots +q^{\,m}.
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Step 1: Each listed linear form divides $\Delta_{n}$.}

Fix $k$ with $1\le k\le 2n+1$ and a tuple
$\boldsymbol i=(i_{1},\dots ,i_{k})\in\mathbb F_{q}^{\,k}$.
Replace the $k$-th column of $\mathcal M_{q,n}(A,B)$
by the $\mathbb F_{q}$-linear combination
\[
  \bigl(F_{k}(\boldsymbol i),F_{k}(\boldsymbol i)^{q},
  \dots ,F_{k}(\boldsymbol i)^{q^{2n+1}}\bigr)^{\!\mathsf T}.
\]
Because the Frobenius map $x\mapsto x^{q}$ is $\mathbb F_{q}$-linear,
this new column is an $\mathbb F_{q}$-linear combination of the first
$k$ columns.  Hence, if $F_{k}(\boldsymbol i)=0$, the modified matrix

gives the factor $c_{0}=a_{0}$.  \hfill$\square$

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Step 2: Equality of the two sides in (1).}

The Moore determinant of size $2n+2$ satisfies
\[
  \deg \Delta_{n}=s_{2n+1}=1+q+\dots +q^{\,2n+1}.
\]
On the right-hand side of (1) every factor is linear,
and their number is exactly $s_{2n+1}$; the two polynomials
have the same total degree.
Since each factor divides $\Delta_{n}$, their product
$\Phi$ divides $\Delta_{n}$, so $\Delta_{n}=\lambda\Phi$
for some $\lambda\in\mathbb F_{q}$.

Consider the monomial
\[
  M:=c_{0}\,c_{1}^{\,q}\,c_{2}^{\,q^{2}}\dotsm c_{2n+1}^{\,q^{\,2n+1}}.
\]
In $\Phi$ there is exactly one way to obtain $M$:
one must pick the leading term $c_{k}$ from the factor
$F_{k}(\boldsymbol i)$ for every $k$, and these leading terms
are pairwise distinct.  Hence the coefficient of $M$ in $\Phi$ is~$1$.

For the determinant $\Delta_{n}$ a Laplace expansion along the first

identity; its sign is $+1$, hence the coefficient of $M$ in
$\Delta_{n}$ is also~$1$.  Consequently $\lambda=1$ and
$\Delta_{n}\equiv\Phi$, proving (1).  \hfill$\square$

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Step 3: Multiplicity one, square-freeness and uniqueness.}

Fix a linear factor
$L:=F_{k}(i_{1},\dots ,i_{k})$ and write
\[
   \Delta_{n}=L\cdot G,
\]
where $G$ denotes the product of all other linear factors in~(1).
Differentiate with respect to $c_{k}$
(which occurs in $L$ with coefficient~$1$):
\[
   \frac{\partial\Delta_{n}}{\partial c_{k}}
   \;=\;
   G\;+\;L\;\frac{\partial G}{\partial c_{k}}.
\]
Reducing the above congruence modulo $L$ gives
$(\partial\Delta_{n}/\partial c_{k})\equiv G$, which is non-zero
because no other factor vanishes identically modulo $L$.
Hence $L\nmid(\partial\Delta_{n}/\partial c_{k})$ and so $L^{2}\nmid
\Delta_{n}$.  Therefore every linear factor occurs with multiplicity~$1$
and $\Delta_{n}$ is square-free modulo~$q$.

Because the factors are pair-wise distinct and each appears once,
any factorisation of $\Delta_{n}$ into linear factors must contain
exactly the same multiset of factors; Step~2 forces the same scalar
multiple, so the factorisation given in (1) is unique.
\hfill$\square$

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Step 4: The coefficient of
$a_{0}\,a_{1}^{\,q}\dotsm a_{n}^{\,q^{\,n}}\,b_{0}$.}

View $\Delta_{n}$ as a polynomial in the variables
$c_{0},c_{1},\dots ,c_{2n+1}$.
A monomial in its expansion is uniquely determined by a
permutation $\sigma$ of $\{0,1,\dots ,2n+1\}$:
the exponent of $c_{j}$ equals
$\sum_{\sigma(i)=j} q^{\,i}$.
Because the powers $q^{\,0},q^{\,1},\dots ,q^{\,2n+1}$
are distinct, this representation is unique.

Suppose the monomial
\[
  N:=a_{0}\,a_{1}^{\,q}\dotsm a_{n}^{\,q^{\,n}}\,b_{0}
      =c_{0}\,c_{1}^{\,q}\dotsm c_{n}^{\,q^{\,n}}\;c_{\,n+1}
\]
appears.  Translating into $c$-variables,
\[
   \deg_{c_{0}}N=1,\;
   \deg_{c_{1}}N=q,\;
   \dots ,
   \deg_{c_{n}}N=q^{\,n},\;
   \deg_{c_{n+1}}N=1,\;
   \deg_{c_{j}}N=0\;(j>n+1).
\]
Because $\deg_{c_{n+1}}N=1$, exactly one row index $r$
satisfies $\sigma(r)=n+1$ and $q^{\,r}=1$, hence $r=0$.
But $\deg_{c_{0}}N=1$ imposes simultaneously $\sigma(0)=0$,
which contradicts $\sigma(0)=n+1$.  Therefore the monomial
$N$ \emph{cannot} occur in $\Delta_{n}(A,B)$, and its coefficient is
\[
  0.
  \qquad\qquad\square
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\textbf{Answer to (4):} the sought coefficient equals $0$.

   more intricate.

   • Part (1) generalises the triangular product to arbitrary n;
   • Part (2) demands familiarity with finite-field extensions and
